text: The surgeon begins mobilization to separate the small bowel from the suspected old pexy site over the sacral promontory on the right side.
The surgeon performs a repeat mobilization of the small bowel, carefully separating it from adhesions likely at the previous pexy site over the sacral promontory on the right.

The surgeon carefully navigates into the retro rectal plane, noting visible scarring in the area likely due to previous dissection and suture pexy to the sacral promontory. The distal anastomosis is also present.
The surgeon navigates below the scarred tissue to reach the normal presacral fascia, where the surgical plane visibly opens up, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon navigates through the surgical field, identifying an area where a previous pexy might have been, as the tissue plane becomes visibly easier to work with.
The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection down to the pelvic floor, carefully navigating around visible scarring and previous surgical alterations.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, visible within the surgical field, ensuring careful dissection to avoid injury.
The surgeon begins the dissection posteriorly before proceeding to the lateral aspect, working within the opened plane over the presacral fascia.
The surgeon attempts to access the medially dissected plane, focusing on the area around the previously identified ureter.
The video shows visible scarring on one side of the surgical field, a result of prior surgery.

The surgeon observes that the gonadal vessels are drawn up onto the left side of the colon conduit down to the anastomosis. The surgeon works to reposition these vessels to access the medially dissected plane.
The ureter is visually identified in the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon has successfully reached and joined the medially dissected plane, completing the dissection necessary for the procedure.
The surgeon continues the left-sided dissection down to the floor, ensuring safe separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and addresses the remaining posterior adhesions in the area where pexy sutures were previously placed.
The surgeon gently pulls down the tissue, which separates easily from the surrounding structures, indicating successful dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the left side down to the floor, preparing to perform a pexy.
The surgeon observes scarring in the posterior region, indicating the likely location of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon identifies a mesenteric hematoma during the dissection, visible as a localized area of blood accumulation within the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate the mesentery, resulting in a visible tear in the tissue.
The surgeon applies pressure to a contained area for approximately five minutes, likely addressing a mesenteric hematoma or tear observed in previous clips.
The surgeon continues with the distal dissection, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect into the rectovaginal septum, carefully navigating the anatomical space between the rectum and vagina.
The surgeon encounters a previously dissected area that is more adherent than usual, with the absence of mesorectum resulting in a lack of the typical fat plane.
The surgeon carefully dissects the sigmoid or descending colon wall, which is positioned directly against the posterior aspect of the vagina, requiring precise manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon leaves the right side lateral stalks intact during the dissection to assist with the placement of the pexy suture.
The surgeon continues the distal dissection, aiming to reach as far down into the rectovaginal septum as possible, navigating the sticky tissue without the usual mesorectal fat plane.
The surgeon palpates the anastomosis site, checking for any abnormalities, although no visible issues are observed externally.
The surgeon completes the dissection on the left side, focusing on separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs additional posterior dissection, carefully separating tissue layers at the back of the surgical site.
The surgeon observes bleeding in the surgical field, noting that it appears to have ruptured through the mesentery.
The surgical field shows no evidence of pulsatile bleeding, indicating stable hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon completes the posterior mobilization of the colon, carefully dissecting and freeing the tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins placing pexy sutures, which are used to fixate or secure tissue in place during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture, likely for securing or repairing tissue during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon checks perfusion in the area where a hematoma is obscuring the view of the bowel wall.
The surgeon observes that the perfusion in the bowel wall appears normal, with no visible signs of compromised blood flow.
The surgeon observes active bleeding from the left lateral side of the bowel while cleaning the surgical area.
The surgeon examines the left aspect of the mesentery, assessing whether the hematoma is egressing through a violation or if there is an active bleeder present.
The surgeon applies focused cautery to control active bleeding on the left aspect of the bowel, where the hematoma is present.
The surgeon attempts to control bleeding by holding a clamp on the affected area of the bowel, successfully stopping the active bleeding.
The surgeon releases the cautery device, revealing continued bleeding from the bowel despite previous attempts to control it.
The surgeon applies hemostatic agents directly to the bleeding site on the bowel and increases pressure to control the bleeding.
The surgeon applies gel foam and thrombin to the bleeding site in an attempt to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy, revealing pink mucosa with no signs of mucosal bleeding.
The surgeon carefully removes hemostatic agents from the surgical site to evaluate the underlying tissue condition.
The surgical field shows active bleeding, possibly from the area where hemostatic agents were previously applied.
The surgeon places a focused suture directly in the area of previous bleeding, which had stopped upon clamping.
The surgeon prepares to perform a maneuver on the mesentery, which is prone to bleeding and friable, making it difficult to hold sutures effectively.
The surgeon uses threo silk sutures to create mattress sutures above and below the area of bleeding in the mesentery.
The surgeon places a new sheet of Surgicel beneath the previously placed mattress sutures to control bleeding in the mesentery.
